50	
51	## Q4 — Hospital ECDs (HiX, Epic NL, Nexus): is there a patient-voice slot?
52	
53	### Readiness card
54	
55	- **Surface.** ChipSoft *HiX* (dominant NL hospital EPD), Epic NL, Nexus. HiX has a *HiX Patient* portal app and a geriatrics module. The geriatrics module supports VMS/GRP screening of patients ≥70 and predefined anamnesis forms (cognitive, falls/mobility, medication review) ([ChipSoft — Geriatrie](https://www.chipsoft.com/nl-NL/hix-abc/artikel/30), [HiX Patient — App Store](https://apps.apple.com/nl/app/hix-pati%C3%ABnt/id1605109373)).
56	- **Patient-voice / personhood / "this is me" slot.** **Not confirmed in public sources for any of HiX, Epic NL, or Nexus.** No public ChipSoft documentation describes a dedicated personhood/preferences/levensverhaal field for dementia or frail-elderly patients. Hospital admission is therefore **a standalone-UI moment for our product, not an adapter moment**, until or unless we partner with a hospital that has built a custom HiX form.
57	- **Cross-vendor exchange.** HiX↔Epic↔Nexus exchange runs on FHIR via the national *(Zorg)platform*; the exchange is BgZ-shaped (clinical summary), not personhood-shaped ([ICT&health — HiX/Epic exchange](https://www.icthealth.nl/nieuws/ziekenhuizen-wisselen-patientdata-uit-tussen-epd-hix-en-epic), [ChipSoft — landelijk Zorgplatform](https://www.chipsoft.com/nl-NL/nieuws/835)).
58	- **Dementia-friendly hospital initiatives.** A 2025 NGN working-group programme on *Dementievriendelijke Ziekenhuiszorg* is active; the *Kennissynthese ziekenhuiszorg dementie* maps gaps and explicitly calls for person-centered approaches and informal-caregiver support, but does not (yet) prescribe a data field in the EPD ([Geheugenpoliklinieken — kennissynthese](https://www.geheugenpoliklinieken.nl/actueel/nieuws/kennissynthese-ziekenhuiszorg-dementie), [Zorgvisie — elf handvatten](https://www.zorgvisie.nl/elf-handvatten-voor-dementievriendelijke-ziekenhuiszorg/)). This is the policy thread to ride for hospital adoption.
59	- **Adapter path.** None today. eOverdracht (the nursing handover standard from VVT into hospitals) does carry social/living-condition zibs and *advance directives* alongside clinical content ([Nictiz eOverdracht v4.0](https://informatiestandaarden.nictiz.nl/wiki/vpk:V4.0_Ontwerp_eOverdracht), [V&VN — eOverdracht factsheet](https://www.venvn.nl/media/megjb1mo/20200824-factsheet-eoverdracht.pdf)) — that gives us a route *into* the hospital via the VVT side rather than a direct HiX integration. By end-2026, every healthcare professional must be able to work with eOverdracht ([V&VN — verpleegkundige overdracht](https://www.venvn.nl/thema-s/vastleggen-en-overdragen/het-overdragen-van-patientgegevens/verpleegkundige-overdracht)).
60	- **Data shape.** eOverdracht is FHIR + zibs. Our PZP-aligned output sits naturally inside an eOverdracht packet.
61	- **Realistic v1 move.** Standalone UI ("ward-handover view") accessible via a QR code on the patient's wristband or family-facing card. No HiX integration in scope.
62	- **Sources.** [ChipSoft — Geriatrie](https://www.chipsoft.com/nl-NL/hix-abc/artikel/30), [HiX Patient](https://www.chipsoft.com/nl-be/hix-abc/hix-abc-articles/hix-patient/), [ICT&health — HiX/Epic exchange](https://www.icthealth.nl/nieuws/ziekenhuizen-wisselen-patientdata-uit-tussen-epd-hix-en-epic), [Geheugenpoliklinieken — kennissynthese](https://www.geheugenpoliklinieken.nl/actueel/nieuws/kennissynthese-ziekenhuiszorg-dementie), [Zorgvisie — elf handvatten](https://www.zorgvisie.nl/elf-handvatten-voor-dementievriendelijke-ziekenhuiszorg/), [Nictiz eOverdracht v4.0](https://informatiestandaarden.nictiz.nl/wiki/vpk:V4.0_Ontwerp_eOverdracht), [V&VN factsheet eOverdracht](https://www.venvn.nl/media/megjb1mo/20200824-factsheet-eoverdracht.pdf).
63	
64	---
65	
66	## Q5 — VVT-side recommended schema
67	
68	### Readiness card
69	
70	- **Vilans — Persoonsgerichte Zorg.** Vilans publishes the *thema* and methodology pages on persoonsgerichte zorg and an article *"Persoonsgerichte zorg op basis van data in ouderenzorg"* — but **no normative schema**, no recommended field set, no zib mapping that an ECD-neutral product is told to align with ([Vilans — persoonsgerichte zorg](https://www.vilans.nl/thema-s/persoonsgerichte-zorg), [Vilans — ECD](https://www.vilans.nl/kennis/ecd-elektronisch-clientendossier)). Confirms persoonsbeeld and levensverhaal as concepts to capture, does not specify how.
71	- **Waardigheid en Trots.** Publishes *Methodiek Persoonsgerichte Zorg* and the *Verhaalcirkel*; the WenT article on ECDs and quality of life uses NANDA / NOC / NIC for problem-and-outcome structuring rather than a personhood schema ([WenT — methodiek persoonsgerichte zorg](https://www.waardigheidentrots.nl/tools/methodiek-persoonsgerichte-zorg/), [WenT — ECD kwaliteit van leven](https://www.waardigheidentrots.nl/praktijk/ecd-kwaliteit-van-leven-en-efficient-werken/)). Not a data schema.
72	- **Zorginstituut — Kwaliteitsstandaard Levensvragen.** Exists, addresses spiritual/meaning questions in care; conceptual rather than data-modelled ([Zorginzicht — Kwaliteitsstandaard Levensvragen PDF](https://www.zorginzicht.nl/binaries/content/assets/zorginzicht/kwaliteitsinstrumenten/Kwaliteitsstandaard+Levensvragen.pdf)).
73	- **V&VN — eOverdracht.** The closest thing to a national VVT data schema for non-clinical patient context. v4.0 is the *minimale eOverdracht*; sections include *algemene leefomstandigheden, sociale omstandigheden, wilsverklaringen* — but no levensverhaal zib and no preference-profile zib ([Nictiz eOverdracht v4.0](https://informatiestandaarden.nictiz.nl/wiki/vpk:V4.0_Ontwerp_eOverdracht), [V&VN secties eOverdracht](https://www.venvn.nl/media/an4iaehg/secties-eoverdracht.pdf)).
74	- **Nictiz — PZP information standard** (covered in Q3) is the **best candidate for the schema we should align with** today: explicitly captures preferences/wishes/values/boundaries, zib-based, FHIR-typed, RC-stage, owned by Nictiz/IKNL, aligned with the Leidraad Proactieve Zorgplanning that NHG/Verenso/V&VN/NVAVG/Patiëntenfederatie all signed.
75	- **InZicht / VIPP.** *VIPP InZicht* is the VWS subsidy that has been pushing VVT/GHZ providers to implement eOverdracht and to disclose data to PGOs by end-2026 ([Beter Healthcare — VIPP InZicht](https://beterhealthcare.nl/vipp-inzicht/), [Drimpy — VIPP InZicht](https://www.drimpy.com/projecten/vipp-inzicht-wat-is-dat-en-waarom/)). It funds plumbing, not new schemas — but the political momentum is the lever to argue "the patient-voice gegevensdienst is the missing brick."
76	- **Bottom line.** **There is no published national schema for *persoonsbeeld* / *levensverhaal* / *zorgvoorkeuren*.** The vacuum is real. The closest standards-grade adjacent thing is **Nictiz PZP**, which we should adopt as our internal data shape and pitch externally as "we ship PZP-conformant output today, before any ECD does."
77	- **Sources.** [Vilans — persoonsgerichte zorg](https://www.vilans.nl/thema-s/persoonsgerichte-zorg), [Vilans — ECD](https://www.vilans.nl/kennis/ecd-elektronisch-clientendossier), [WenT — methodiek persoonsgerichte zorg](https://www.waardigheidentrots.nl/tools/methodiek-persoonsgerichte-zorg/), [WenT — ECD kwaliteit van leven](https://www.waardigheidentrots.nl/praktijk/ecd-kwaliteit-van-leven-en-efficient-werken/), [Kwaliteitsstandaard Levensvragen](https://www.zorginzicht.nl/binaries/content/assets/zorginzicht/kwaliteitsinstrumenten/Kwaliteitsstandaard+Levensvragen.pdf), [Nictiz PZP](https://nationalebibliotheek.nictiz.nl/bibliotheek/proactieve-zorgplanning-pzp/), [VIPP InZicht — Beter Healthcare](https://beterhealthcare.nl/vipp-inzicht/), [VIPP InZicht — Drimpy](https://www.drimpy.com/projecten/vipp-inzicht-wat-is-dat-en-waarom/).
78	
79	---
80	
81	## Prioritised integration roadmap — next 6 months
82	
83	1. **Nedap *Mijn verhaal* (paste-in v1, partner-mediated write v2).** Highest distribution leverage in NL VVT and home-care; the slot is in market and currently underused (editing disabled). Move first because procurement officers already know the field name. Path: ship the structured-export today, open conversations with Creq / Quattri / Infozorg, target a partner-mediated write within the InZicht funding window.
84	2. **PinkRoccade mijnCaress *persoonsbeeld* (FHIR-friendly v1).** Second VVT vendor, more API-friendly. Use the explicit ZIB/iStandaarden v5.4 framing to push a FHIR-PZP-shaped write into persoonsbeeld. Likely faster to a real API write than Nedap.
85	3. **Nictiz PZP reference implementation.** Adopt PZP zibs/FHIR as our internal data shape. As PZP graduates from RC to production, position to be the reference implementation and the canonical authoring tool. This is the lever that turns a "yet another startup" pitch into "we are shipping the standard."
86	4. **MedMij PGO export (Ivido / Drimpy / Quli).** Ship a FHIR-PZP bundle as a MedMij-style download today; lobby for a *Levensverhaal en Zorgvoorkeuren* gegevensdienst. Not until 2027 realistically.
87	5. **eOverdracht handoff into hospitals.** Add the patient-voice content to outbound eOverdracht packets so hospitals receive it whether or not HiX has a slot. Quietly the most scalable way into hospitals.
88	6. **Hospital-direct (HiX, Epic, Nexus).** Standalone UI / QR-on-wristband mode only. Revisit when a dementia-vriendelijke ziekenhuiszorg pilot site asks.
89	
90	## Three realistic adapter paths for this weekend's demo
91	
92	1. **Structured PDF + markdown export shaped to *Mijn verhaal*** (aanspreekvorm, voorkeuren, narrative, photo placeholder), pasted by family or casemanager into Caren. Reads as "fills the field Nedap already ships."
93	2. **FHIR R4 PZP-shaped resource bundle**, downloadable from the standalone UI. Reads as "MedMij-grade output today, pre-aligned with the Nictiz Proactieve Zorgplanning standard."
94	3. **Standalone web view with QR-code share link**, gated by patient/representative consent — covers the hospital admission, locum nurse, and family-without-portal cases. Reads as "works on day one, in any setting, no integration required."
95	
96	## Policy / standards thread to ride
97	
98	**Nictiz Informatiestandaard Proactieve Zorgplanning (PZP)** is the single most leveraged thread. It is FHIR-R4, zib-based, owned by Nictiz/IKNL, signed by the major professional bodies, currently RC-stage, and conceptually one-to-one with our six wedges. If we ship PZP-conformant output now and become the de-facto authoring tool while the standard is still hardening, we get vendor-neutral distribution faster than chasing each ECD. The companion lever is **VIPP InZicht / end-2026 eOverdracht mandate** — we ride the funding that is already pushing VVT providers to expose structured data, and we argue that the patient-voice gegevensdienst is the obvious next brick MedMij is missing.
99

1	# HCP/Caretaker Frontend MVP
2	
3	## Product Role
4	
5	Next.js webapp for healthcare professionals and caretakers to understand a patient from the top down: current state, care passport, recent evidence, risk signals, and a query interface for the memory assistant. The knowledge layer is separate; the frontend consumes structured summaries, citations, timeline events, alerts, and answer objects.
6	
7	Clinical boundary: this is decision support, not a medical device claim, diagnosis engine, care-order tool, or legal representative. Every answer must show evidence and route responsibility back to a human professional.
8	
9	## UX Principles From Research
10	
11	- CDS should be timely, person-specific, clear, workflow-fit, and presented in the right format/channel/time. Use this as the organizing rule for alerts, summaries, and query answers.
12	- Keep AI inspectable: show what is known, why it is believed, source strength, recency, and uncertainty. Do not hide behind a fluent answer.
13	- Separate patient voice from system inference. Label verbatim patient statements, clinician-entered facts, caregiver notes, and model summaries differently.
14	- Support correction and auditability. HCPs must be able to flag wrong memories, suggest corrections, and see unresolved contradictions.
15	- Reduce alert fatigue. Only surface risks that are recent, actionable, and tied to evidence; let lower-confidence patterns live in the timeline.
16	- Design for clinical safety: patient identity is always visible, alerts avoid ambiguous language, destructive actions require confirmation, and provenance is one click away.
17	
18	Primary references used: FDA Clinical Decision Support Software guidance (Jan 2026), ONC Clinical Decision Support and SAFER Guides, AHRQ patient-centered CDS / "5 Rights", NHS England DCB0129/DCB0160 clinical risk management standards, WHO AI for health ethics guidance, NIST AI Risk Management Framework, NHS Service Manual accessibility guidance.
19	
20	## Information Architecture
21	
22	Single-patient MVP. Use a left rail plus patient header.
23	
24	- `Overview` - cockpit for shift handover and first 30 seconds.
25	- `Care Passport` - stable preferences, values, routines, communication guidance.
26	- `Ask Memory` - query assistant with answer cards and evidence drawer.
27	- `Timeline` - voice notes, check-ins, events, corrections, alerts.
28	- `Risks` - actionable care gaps and escalation state.
29	- `Memory Review` - inspect/correct extracted memories and contradictions.
30	
31	Persistent header:
32	
33	- Patient name, preferred name, photo/initials, age, condition stage, living situation.
34	- Identity safety strip: date of birth, address/care setting, NHS/BSN/mock identifier.
35	- Status chips: `Updated today`, `3 open risks`, `2 unreviewed corrections`.
36	- Mode label: `Decision support only - verify before acting`.
37	
38	## Key Screens
39	
40	### 1. Patient Overview / Cockpit
41	
42	Purpose: let an HCP understand Anna quickly before a visit, discharge planning call, or care meeting.
43	
44	Layout:
45	
46	- Top row: patient identity, last contact, confidence/coverage meter, primary concerns.
47	- `What matters to Anna` summary: 3-5 patient-centered bullets.
48	- `Today / This week` panel: relevant recent changes, missed check-ins, mood/sleep/appetite/routine deviations.
49	- `Ask about Anna` query box with suggested prompts.
50	- `Open risks` compact list with severity, evidence count, last observed, owner/status.
51	- `Care guidance` cards: communication, routines, triggers, medication adherence support, mobility/safety notes.
52	- `Evidence freshness` footer: last voice note, last reviewed memory, pending contradictions.
53	
54	Cockpit design rule: no dense EHR clone. It should feel like a clinical handover board: scannable, calm, high signal.
55	
56	### 2. Care Passport
57	
58	Purpose: structured view of the patient's voice and care preferences.
59	
60	Sections:
61	
62	- Identity and life context: preferred name, language, important people/places, former occupation, values.
63	- Communication: preferred tone, topics that calm/distress, cognitive aids, hearing/vision needs.
64	- Daily routines: morning/evening, meals, hygiene, sleep, mobility, transport.
65	- Preferences: food, activities, privacy, social contact, spiritual/cultural needs.
66	- Medical-care values: comfort vs intervention preferences, fears, acceptable tradeoffs. Label as `not an advance directive`.
67	- Safety: wandering triggers, falls, medication problems, emergency contacts, environment notes.
68	- Evidence map per field: source type, date range, confidence, reviewer status.
69	
70	Each passport field has:
71	
72	- `statement`: human-readable summary.
73	- `patient_voice`: short quote/transcript snippet when available.
74	- `sources`: linked evidence items.
75	- `confidence`: high/medium/low plus reason.
76	- `last_confirmed_at`.
77	- `review_status`: unreviewed / reviewed / disputed / corrected.
78	
79	### 3. Ask Memory
80	
81	Purpose: let HCPs ask natural questions while keeping answers bounded and evidenced.
82	
83	Example prompts:
84	
85	- "What helps Anna calm down when she is confused?"
86	- "Does Anna prefer care at home if her condition worsens?"
87	- "What changed in Anna's routine this week?"
88	- "What should I avoid during morning visits?"
89	- "Is there evidence Anna is missing medication?"
90	
91	Answer card:
92	
93	- Direct answer first, max 4-6 lines.
94	- Safety label: `Preference support`, `Care context`, `Risk pattern`, or `Insufficient evidence`.
95	- Evidence strength: number of sources, date span, consistency, confidence.
96	- `Why this answer` bullets separating patient statements, observed patterns, and inferred summary.
97	- Citations with source chips: `Voice note`, `WhatsApp check-in`, `Caregiver note`, `Clinician correction`.
98	- Contradictions/uncertainty box when relevant.
99	- Actions: `Open evidence`, `Add to handover`, `Flag as wrong`, `Ask follow-up`, `Copy summary`.
100	
101	Answer constraints:
102	
103	- Never answer as if Anna is currently consenting to a medical/legal decision.
104	- Prefer "Anna has previously said..." over "Anna wants..." unless recent and repeatedly confirmed.
105	- If evidence is stale or weak, say so visibly.
106	- For urgent risk questions, show escalation guidance as `consider contacting care team`, not automated triage.
107	
108	### 4. Evidence / Provenance Display
109	
110	Evidence is not a footnote; it is part of the core UI.
111	
112	Evidence drawer contents:
113	
114	- Source list grouped by claim.
115	- Transcript excerpt with timestamp and audio playback placeholder.
116	- Captured date, channel, speaker, language, transcription confidence.
117	- Extraction path: source -> memory item -> summary/answer.
118	- Reviewer history: who reviewed/corrected and when.
119	- Contradictions: competing statements side by side.
120	
121	Visual language:
122	
123	- Use source chips and icons, not long raw IDs in the main card.
124	- Use confidence as plain language with reasons, not only a percentage.
125	- Show provenance depth progressively: overview chip -> evidence drawer -> raw transcript.
126	
127	### 5. Timeline And Risk Alerts
128	
129	Timeline filters:
130	
131	- All, voice notes, check-ins, routines, care events, risks, corrections.
132	- Date range and confidence filter.
133	
134	Timeline item anatomy:
135	
136	- Event title, date/time, source, summary, linked memories, confidence, review state.
137	- For voice notes: short transcript snippet and audio placeholder.
138	- For corrections: before/after and reviewer.
139	
140	Risk alerts:
141	
142	- `Medication adherence concern`: missed/refused mentions, timing drift, evidence links.
143	- `Nutrition/hydration concern`: reduced appetite/drinking mentions.
144	- `Mood/social isolation`: repeated loneliness/anxiety markers.
145	- `Safety at home`: falls, getting lost, stove/door concerns.
146	- `Care preference conflict`: new statement contradicts existing passport field.
147	
148	Risk alert anatomy:
149	
150	- Severity: info / watch / action needed.
151	- Evidence count and recency.
152	- Why surfaced now.
153	- Suggested human next step.
154	- Owner/status: new / acknowledged / in progress / resolved / dismissed.
155	
156	### 6. Memory Inspection / Correction
157	
158	Purpose: make the memory layer editable and accountable without pretending HCPs are directly editing raw model truth.
159	
160	Workflow:
161	
162	1. HCP opens `Memory Review`.
163	2. Unreviewed memories appear as cards grouped by passport category or risk.
164	3. HCP can `Confirm`, `Flag wrong`, `Edit summary`, `Mark outdated`, or `Request patient follow-up`.
165	4. Correction form requires reason and optional replacement wording.
166	5. System stores correction as an overlay event; original source remains available.
167	6. Corrected memory updates dependent passport fields, answers, and risk cards.
168	
169	Correction object states:
170	
171	- `pending_review`
172	- `accepted`
173	- `rejected`
174	- `needs_patient_followup`
175	- `superseded`
176	
177	## Demo Scenario: Anna
178	
179	Anna is 74, lives alone, early dementia, no nearby family. She has used the WhatsApp voice companion for several weeks.
180	
181	Demo flow:
182	
183	1. HCP opens Anna's cockpit before a home visit.
184	2. Overview shows: values independence, dislikes being rushed, calms with Dutch oldies and tea, recent missed evening check-ins, possible medication timing drift.
185	3. HCP asks: "What should I know before discussing extra home support?"
186	4. Answer card says Anna fears "being put away", prefers help framed as staying independent at home, and responds better to concrete examples than abstract future planning.
187	5. HCP opens evidence: two voice notes from Anna, one repeated routine pattern, one low-confidence contradiction about whether she wants a cleaner.
188	6. HCP flags the cleaner preference as uncertain and requests a patient follow-up question.
189	7. Risk tab shows `watch`: missed evening medication mentions in 3 of 5 recent days; suggested next step is medication reconciliation with district nurse.
190	8. Care Passport exports/prints a one-page handover summary for the care meeting.
191	
192	## Next.js Prototype Structure
193	
194	Keep implementation demoable and local-first. Mock the API; do not build the knowledge layer.
195	
196	```text
197	code/hcp-frontend/
198	  app/
199	    layout.tsx
200	    page.tsx                         # redirect to /patients/anna
201	    patients/[patientId]/layout.tsx   # patient header + nav
202	    patients/[patientId]/page.tsx     # overview cockpit
203	    patients/[patientId]/passport/page.tsx
204	    patients/[patientId]/ask/page.tsx
205	    patients/[patientId]/timeline/page.tsx
206	    patients/[patientId]/risks/page.tsx
207	    patients/[patientId]/memory/page.tsx
208	  components/
209	    PatientHeader.tsx
210	    PatientNav.tsx
211	    OverviewCockpit.tsx
212	    CarePassportSection.tsx
213	    AskMemoryPanel.tsx
214	    AnswerCard.tsx
215	    EvidenceDrawer.tsx
216	    TimelineList.tsx
217	    RiskAlertCard.tsx
218	    MemoryReviewCard.tsx
219	    ConfidenceBadge.tsx
220	    SourceChip.tsx
221	  lib/
222	    mock-api.ts
223	    types.ts
224	    anna-demo-data.ts
225	```
226	
227	Use server components for page shell and static mock data. Use client components only for query input, drawers, filters, and correction interactions.
228	
229	## Mock API Contracts
230	
231	```ts
232	type Patient = {
233	  id: string;
234	  fullName: string;
235	  preferredName: string;
236	  age: number;
237	  dateOfBirth: string;

244

247	  category: "identity" | "communication" | "routine" | "preferences" | "values" | "safety";
248	  title: string;

251	  confidence: "high" | "medium" | "low";

253	  lastConfirmedAt?: string;

256	};
257	
258	type EvidenceSource = {

316	
317	Mock endpoints:
318	
319	- `GET /api/patients/anna`
320	- `GET /api/patients/anna/overview`
321	- `GET /api/patients/anna/passport`
322	- `POST /api/patients/anna/query`
323	- `GET /api/patients/anna/evidence?ids=...`
324	- `GET /api/patients/anna/timeline`
325	- `GET /api/patients/anna/risks`
326	- `POST /api/patients/anna/memory-corrections`
327	
328	## Visual / Design Principles
329	
330	- Serious healthcare demo: restrained palette, high contrast, generous spacing, no playful gradients, no chat-bot mascots.
331	- Use clinical colors sparingly: red only for action-needed safety risks, amber for watch, blue/gray for informational support.
332	- Make the patient the focus: name, story, voice snippets, and care-relevant context should be more prominent than AI branding.
333	- Dense enough for clinicians, but not an EHR replica. Prioritize handover cards, tables only where comparison matters.
334	- Accessibility: WCAG 2.2 AA target, keyboard-accessible drawers/tabs, visible focus states, no color-only risk encoding.
335	- Use clear labels: `Evidence`, `Uncertainty`, `Human next step`, `Last confirmed`, `Not an advance directive`.
336	- Avoid overconfidence: never show a single "AI score" as the main decision signal.
337	
338	## 24h Build Priorities
339	
340	1. Cockpit page with Anna demo data and visible safety boundary.
341	2. Ask Memory page with 3-4 canned questions returning rich answer cards.
342	3. Evidence drawer that links answer claims to mock voice-note excerpts.
343	4. Care Passport page with field-level confidence/review state.
344	5. Risks page with 2-3 actionable alerts and human next steps.
345	6. Memory Review page with local-only correction state.
346	7. One-click `Handover summary` mock export view if time permits.

1	# Knowledge Layer (lightweight)
2	
3	Purpose: preserve the patient's voice as evidence-backed memory that HCPs and caretakers can query later. The system supports human judgement; it does not make legal, medical, or capacity decisions.
4	
5	**Decision (2026-04-25):** dropped Postgres / pgvector / Supabase from the prototype. Too much DB ceremony for a 24-hour demo. The data model below is conceptual; the prototype stores everything as JSON files per patient and does retrieval in-process.
6	
7	## Design Principles
8	
9	- Store raw evidence first. Every generated memory must point back to a transcript span (audio ID + start/end) so a clinician can verify with the patient's actual words.
10	- Separate memory types. Don't collapse quotes, preferences, events, dates, and risk state into one summary blob.
11	- Append-only evidence + small derived views. The care passport can be regenerated; the transcript cannot.
12	- Show uncertainty by default. Answers should say "based on X notes" or "unclear/conflicting" rather than sounding authoritative.
13	- Keep the build boring: JSON files, LLM structured output, a single processing pass per voice note, retrieval by feeding the relevant slice to the LLM with a citation requirement.
14	
15	## Memory Taxonomy (conceptual, not a schema)
16	
17	1. **Raw transcript evidence** — the audio + transcript + chunk-level metadata. Source of truth, never overwritten.
18	2. **Semantic profile** — current best-known facts and preferences (identity, communication style, food, routines, fears, values, people/places, care preferences). Each item carries evidence links and confidence.
19	3. **Episodic timeline** — dated or approximate life/care events (falls, appointments, missed meds, anxiety episodes, family mentions, meaningful memories). Append-only.
20	4. **Temporal facts** — facts with validity windows or recurrence ("physio every Tuesday", "daughter visits monthly"). Recency matters.
21	5. **Risk / care-gap signals** — derived, non-diagnostic flags (missed meals, medication confusion, isolation, repeated distress). Always framed as "possible care gap signals", never clinical diagnosis.
22	
23	## Prototype Storage (24h) — markdown-with-tails
24	
25	**Decision (2026-04-25):** the structured layer is **semi-formal markdown**, one file per domain per patient, with structured tails on every fact. JSON is reserved for raw transcripts and the episodic timeline. No vector DB, no Postgres.
26	
27	Why this shape:
28	- LLMs produce markdown-with-tails natively; they fight strict JSON when the content is nuanced ("he calls his wife mama" doesn't fit any pre-written schema).
29	- Humans can audit at a glance — every domain file opens in any editor.
30	- The same markdown pastes into Caren *Mijn verhaal* (strip the tails) and parses into FHIR PZP resources (read the tails).
31	- Demoable: in the pitch you can show the markdown updating live as a voice note is processed.
32	
33	Folder per patient:
34	
35	```
36	data/patients/<patient_id>/
37	  README.md                   # one-paragraph who-is-this
38	  personhood.md               # identity, life chapters, language, faith
39	  care-preferences.md         # bathing, food, communication, hygiene
40	  acp/
41	    01-daily-pleasure.md      # what makes life worth living
42	    02-daily-care.md          # how I want to be helped
43	    03-treatment-wishes.md    # hospital, medication, escalation ceilings
44	    04-coping-end-of-life.md  # how I cope, end-of-life wishes
45	    05-finances-legal.md      # money, mandate, representation
46	    06-housing-network.md     # where, with whom, social network
47	  hot-moments.md              # known triggers and calmers — Story 3 lives here
48	  social-network.md           # who matters, who to call (priority order)
49	  signals.md                  # things to watch, framed as observations
50	  timeline.jsonl              # append-only episodic events
51	  notes/
52	    n_001.json                # raw transcript + chunks (immutable)
53	    n_002.json
54	```
55	
56	Each markdown file holds bullets with structured tails:
57	
58	```markdown
59	# Care preferences — Greet de Vries
60	
61	## Bathing
62	- Always start the wash with the left arm — starting on the right causes panic. {confidence: high, sources: [n_004#c_2], first_seen: 2026-04-12, last_confirmed: 2026-04-21}
63	- Hum the Wilhelmus during the wash — relaxes within seconds. {confidence: high, sources: [n_004#c_8, n_011#c_3]}
64	- Prefers a female caregiver for intimate care. {confidence: medium, sources: [n_007#c_1]}
65	
66	## Eating and drinking
67	- Tea before breakfast, always. Coffee causes panic. {confidence: high, sources: [n_001#c_4, n_009#c_2]}
68	- Won't eat off green plates. {confidence: medium, sources: [family-2026-04-19], attributed: family/Mark}
69	```
70	
71	Tail conventions:
72	- `confidence`: `high | medium | low`
73	- `sources`: list of `<note_id>#<chunk_id>` for patient-authored, or `<channel>-<date>` for family/staff
74	- `first_seen`, `last_confirmed`: ISO dates
75	- `status`: `active | needs_review | superseded` (default `active`)
76	- `attributed`: `family/<name>` or `staff/<role>` when not patient-authored
77	- `by`: chunk ID of the bullet that supersedes this one
78	
79	## Processing Per Voice Note
80	
81	1. **Ingest**: receive audio (WhatsApp webhook OR mock upload). Save the file. Create `notes/<note_id>.json` with status `received`.
82	2. **Transcribe**: speech-to-text with chunking (sentence-level or ~30s segments with start/end timestamps and stable chunk IDs).
83	3. **Extract**: one LLM call with the patient's existing markdown files in context. Output is a *patch*: which markdown files to update, which new bullets to add, which existing bullets to update (`last_confirmed`) or supersede.
84	4. **Apply patch**: write the markdown updates. Append to `timeline.jsonl` for episodic events. Conflicts stay as two bullets with `status: needs_review`.
85	5. **Done**: no separate "refresh views" step — the markdown *is* the view.
86	
87	Run synchronously behind a "processing…" spinner. No queue.
88	
89	## Retrieval For HCP Questions
90	
91	1. **Classify** the question: preference, routine, values, recent events, risk, or out-of-scope (medical/legal decision → refuse).
92	2. **Pull** the relevant markdown slice: which domain files to load (e.g., a "what calms her" question loads `hot-moments.md` + `care-preferences.md`). Read recent timeline + raw transcript chunks if the question is about events or quotes.
93	3. **Answer** with one LLM call that must cite source IDs and quote the patient. Refuse if evidence is thin.
94	4. **Log** the question, answer, and cited sources for audit.
95	
96	Answer shape:
97	
98	```json
99	{
100	  "answer": "She appears to prefer tea before breakfast and quieter mornings.",
101	  "confidence": "medium",
102	  "evidence": [
103	    {"date": "2026-04-21", "quote": "I always feel better if I have my tea first.", "note_id": "n_123", "chunk_id": "c_4"}
104	  ],
105	  "uncertainty": "No evidence from the last 7 days confirms this still holds.",
106	  "safety_boundary": "Preference support, not a medical instruction."
107	}
108	```
109	
110	## Safety Boundaries
111	
112	- Every memory carries confidence and evidence count; low-confidence items are visible as "needs review", not care-passport truth.
113	- Conflicts: keep both sides with dates and quotes. UI says "conflicting evidence" rather than picking silently.
114	- Recency: profile answers weight `last_confirmed_at`; risk answers default to last 7–30 days.
115	- No source-less generation: every fact in the profile must trace to at least one transcript citation.
116	- Out-of-scope: refuse questions asking the AI to decide capacity, consent, treatment, discharge, medication changes, or legal representation.
117	- Privacy: per-patient data only; no cross-patient retrieval.
118	- Voice preservation: keep the patient's actual words in citations; don't only render clinical paraphrases.
119	
120	## Stack (minimal)
121	
122	- **Frontend**: Next.js App Router + Tailwind + shadcn/ui — single page with patient picker, care-passport view, Q&A box.
123	- **Backend**: Next.js API routes. No separate service.
124	- **LLM**: OpenAI or Anthropic SDK directly. Structured outputs for extraction and Q&A.
125	- **Speech**: OpenAI transcription (`gpt-4o-mini-transcribe`).
126	- **Storage**: local filesystem during the hackathon. (If hosting requires it, write to /tmp or an S3-like blob — but data is ephemeral demo data anyway.)
127	- **No DB. No vector store. No auth beyond a fake role switcher.**
128	
129	## What To Build In 24h
130	
131	Implement:
132	- Audio upload (or pasted transcript) → `notes/n_xxx.json` + a markdown patch applied to the patient folder.
133	- A `/api/passport/query` endpoint that classifies, retrieves the right markdown slice, and answers with citations. This is the single integration surface for both the dashboard (Luc) and the voice agent (Pieter).
134	- 2–3 seeded patients with rich transcripts so each user-story demo lands.
135	
136	The dashboard, voice agent, and any export adapters all read from the same patient folder + call the same query API. Markdown is the source of truth.
137	
138	Fake or skip:
139	- WhatsApp production integration → mock webhook or file upload.
140	- Diarization → seeded single-speaker transcripts.
141	- Conflict resolution → mark and show both sides.
142	- Risk scoring → LLM-labeled "possible care gap signals", no numbers.
143	- Consent/legal workflow → status badge + boundary copy, no real legal docs.
144	- Multi-patient scale → don't.
145

1	# Story 4 — "How does this knowledge get in?"
2	
3	**One-liner:** The patient's own voice is the primary input; family and caregivers can contribute, clearly labelled as secondary, with provenance preserved everywhere.
4	
5	## The moment
6	
7	Tuesday morning. A WhatsApp message arrives on a patient's phone: *"Good morning. What is one thing you're looking forward to today?"* She records a 40-second voice note while making tea. Forty minutes later her care passport has updated: a new preference ("morning tea before any care"), a new mention of her late husband, a new fragment about the garden she misses.
8	
9	Saturday afternoon. Her son visits. He notices she's been calling the dog by her father's name. He opens the family channel and adds a short note: *"She's been calling the dog 'Henk' this week — that was her father's name."* The system saves it as a family contribution, not a patient statement.
10	
11	Wednesday shift. The home-care nurse notices the patient flinches when approached from the right. She taps a quick "I noticed today" entry into the staff app. It saves as a caregiver observation with her name attached.
12	
13	Three months later, a new locum nurse opens the briefing card and sees all three pieces of evidence — patient-authored, family-contributed, staff-observed — clearly labelled and source-linked.
14	
15	## Who's in the scene
16	
17	- **The patient (primary):** still capable of self-expression, willing to spend a minute or two a day talking to the system. The author of their own story.
18	- **The informal caregiver / family (secondary):** loving, present, but not the patient. Their input adds context the patient can't or won't share, but it is not the patient's voice.
19	- **The professional caregiver (tertiary):** observes things in real time that the patient may not articulate. Their input is structured, attributed, and treated as observation.
20	- **The system (passive):** never makes things up, never blends sources silently.
21	
22	## What they need from us
23	
24	> *"Make it easy to contribute. Make it obvious who said what. Never let one source pretend to be another."*
25	
26	### From the patient
27	
28	- **Light-touch prompts on a familiar channel** — a weekly WhatsApp question, then occasional follow-ups based on gaps.
29	- **Spontaneous notes welcome** — they can send a voice note any time without a prompt.
30	- **No app to install.** Voice notes only — no typing required, no UI to learn. (Phone-call fallback for those who don't use WhatsApp.)
31	- **Visible consent state.** They can see what's stored, withdraw items, or pause the system.
32	
33	### From family
34	
35	- **A separate, clearly-labelled channel** — a different WhatsApp number, or a tagged web form. Their contributions are stored as `source: family/<name>`, not as patient-authored.
36	- **Acknowledgement that this is supplementary.** The UI never elevates a family note above a patient note on the same topic without flagging the conflict.
37	
38	### From caregivers
39	
40	- **Two-tap input on shift** — "I noticed today that..." → speech-to-text → tagged with their role and name.
41	- **Non-clinical phrasing** — caregiver observations are stored as observations, not as patient preferences.
42	
43	## What we show
44	
45	- **Patient-facing:** WhatsApp conversation, voice-only. The system asks, the patient responds. Friendly, slow, no jargon.
46	- **Family-facing:** a dedicated channel (separate WhatsApp number or web form) for adding context. Always asks "is this something *they* said, or something *you* observed?"
47	- **Caregiver-facing:** in-app quick-entry on the standalone view; voice or text; auto-attributed.
48	- **Across all surfaces:** every captured item is tagged with **who said it, when, in what channel, and with what consent state.** No exception.
49	
50	## What we explicitly aren't
51	
52	- **Not silent recording.** No background listening, no ambient mics, no continuous capture. Every input is consciously initiated.
53	- **Not family-authored-by-default.** The patient's voice is the primary trust currency. Family input is real but always labelled.
54	- **Not a free-text dump.** Every input goes through extraction with provenance — even a casual voice note becomes structured items with quotes and timestamps.
55	- **Not editable past.** Corrections create new versions; they don't overwrite. The transcript is immutable.
56	- **Not consent-once.** Consent is granular (who can see what), revisitable, and revocable.
57	
58	## Demo / pitch angle
59	
60	This story is **the trust foundation of the entire pitch.** Without it, the other three stories raise the question "where does this data come from and who can I trust?"
61	
62	Demo it briefly but explicitly. Show:
63	1. A patient sending a 30-second WhatsApp voice note.
64	2. The system extracting two structured items, each with a source clip.
65	3. The same items appearing in the briefing card from Story 1, citable to the patient's own voice.
66	
67	The line: **"Every piece of knowledge in here came from a real person, on the record, with consent. We don't blend, we don't infer, we don't replace anyone's voice with our own."**
68	
69	This is also where the **regulatory and ethical posture** lives: GDPR-grade consent, NEN 7510 controls, EU hosting, source-cited output, human sign-off before anything reaches the formal care record. The seller should have one slide that summarises this stack — it converts skepticism into trust.
70

1	# Story 3 — "She's screaming and I don't know why"
2	
3	**One-liner:** Mid-incident, a caregiver gets a fast, person-specific answer about what has worked before — within seconds, not menus.
4	
5	## The moment
6	
7	It is 7:15 a.m. The night-shift nurse is mid-wash with a patient who has just begun screaming and pulling away. The nurse has tried the things that usually work. Nothing is working. The chart says "Alzheimer's, mild." That doesn't help right now.
8	
9	Or: it's 3 a.m. on a hospital ward. A confused patient is trying to pull out an IV, calling for someone the night nurse doesn't know. The notes say "agitated post-op." That doesn't help either.
10	
11	Or: a daughter is alone with her mother who has just had a panic attack. She doesn't know whether to call an ambulance.
12	
13	In every case, the caregiver has **30 to 90 seconds** before the situation either resolves or escalates. They don't have time for a portal login, a search box, or a five-tab medical record.
14	
15	## Who's in the scene
16	
17	- **The caregiver:** stress-loaded, hands often busy, possibly also trying to physically support the patient. Not in a state to read paragraphs.
18	- **The patient:** in distress, not articulate, possibly frightened of the very person trying to help.
19	- **The wider team (absent):** the colleague who knows the patient's calmers is off shift; the family member who would know is asleep or unreachable.
20	
21	## What they need from us
22	
23	> *"In 10 seconds: what has worked before? What should I stop doing? Who do I call if this doesn't resolve?"*
24	
25	Specifically:
26	- **Top 3 calmers** ranked by past success in the patient's own voice notes ("my garden", "Wilhelmus humming", "tea before anything else").
27	- **Top 3 known agitators** to remove from the situation ("mirrors", "starting from the right side", "calling her by her first name").
28	- **One soothing phrase or object** the patient has explicitly named.
29	- **One named contact** to call if it doesn't resolve (priority order, with role).
30	- **Optional: a 30-second clip of the patient's own voice** on a topic that has calmed them historically. Played quietly nearby, this can break the loop.
31	
32	## What we show
33	
34	A single, large-button "Right now they're upset" mode. One tap from the home screen.
35	
36	The screen is mobile-first, voice-callable, designed to be readable at arm's length while one hand is occupied:
37	- 3 big cards: **TRY**, **AVOID**, **CALL**.
38	- A **PLAY HER VOICE** button at the bottom — one tap, no menus, plays the calmest clip we have.
39	- A subtle row at the very bottom: "Did this help?" → one-tap feedback that re-ranks calmers over time.
40	
41	Form factor: a dedicated mobile route (or PWA) optimised for one-thumb operation. Should also be reachable from a WhatsApp message: "*hot* [patient name]" returns the same three cards as a chat reply.
42	
43	## What we explicitly aren't
44	
45	- **Not a clinical de-escalation tool.** We surface what's worked for *this person* historically. We do not provide BPSD management protocols, sedation guidance, or restraint advice.
46	- **Not a crisis line.** If the situation involves harm or doesn't resolve, the named contact and the standard emergency route are the fallback.
47	- **Not a behavioural-prediction model.** We don't predict why she's upset; we surface what's helped before.
48	- **Not authoritative.** The caregiver is in the room; we are not. The cards are *suggestions from her own voice*, not orders.
49	
50	## Demo / pitch angle
51	
52	This is the **emotional climax of the pitch.** Person-centered care wins over the chart in a single moment. The setup is the hospital admission from Story 1; the payoff is this:
53	
54	> *"At 3 a.m. she starts pulling at her IV. The night nurse opens her phone. Card one: 'Try humming the Wilhelmus — it has calmed her three times.' Card two: 'Avoid mirrors and bright overhead lights.' Card three: 'Call her son Mark — he can be on the phone in two minutes.' She hums. The patient stops crying. The IV stays in."*
55	
56	The line: **"The chart said she was agitated. The passport said how to reach her."**
57

Read. This changes the framing a lot — and surfaces an architecture call we need to make consciously.

## What's now clear about the wedge

This is **not** a generic patient-facts knowledge graph. It's specifically:

- **Tacit/personhood capture for frail elders + early-stage dementia in NL home-care/VVT.** The ECD already owns meds, ICD codes, vitals — we explicitly *do not* duplicate that.
- The data being captured is the "magic-hands" stuff: *"hum the Wilhelmus during the wash", "calls husband 'mama'", "tea before breakfast or she panics", "approach from the left, deaf side"*.
- The schema gravity well already exists: **Nictiz PZP** (6 ACP wedges) + Kitwood personhood + the *Mijn verhaal* slot in Caren we want to feed.
- The killer demo moment is **Story 3** (hot-moment): top-3 calmers / top-3 agitators / one named contact, in <10 seconds, mid-incident.

## The architecture conflict

Today's `knowledge-layer.md` decision (also dated 2026-04-25) says:

> *"dropped Postgres / pgvector / Supabase from the prototype. Too much DB ceremony for a 24-hour demo. … semi-formal **markdown-with-tails**, one file per domain per patient."*

We then spent the morning building **Graphiti + Kuzu + Voyage + Temporal + Pydantic entities**. That's the exact "DB ceremony" the knowledge-layer.md decision was rejecting.

I should have asked before going down the Graphiti path. Now the call:

| | Markdown-with-tails | Graphiti KG |
|---|---|---|
| Demoability | live-updates on screen during pitch | needs UI to render — invisible by default |
| Caren *Mijn verhaal* paste-in | strip tails → done | needs a renderer |
| PZP FHIR export | parse tails → done | needs a renderer |
| Story 3 hot-moment retrieval | LLM reads `hot-moments.md` slice — <2s, fine at this scale | hybrid search, ~150ms — overkill for ~50 facts/patient |
| Temporal validity / contradictions | hand-coded `status: superseded` tails | first-class `valid_at` / `invalid_at` |
| Auditability for clinicians | every fact visible in any text editor | needs the HCP frontend to inspect |
| Provenance (`sources: [n_004#c_2]`) | tails | edge metadata |
| Build cost (24h budget) | very low — already designed | medium — already built but adds runtime |

**My recommendation**: pivot back to **markdown-with-tails as source of truth** for the demo. Keep Temporal+FastAPI shape (per-patient workflow, signal handler for incoming voice notes, fast HCP-facing API). Drop Graphiti from the demo critical path. The scaffold isn't wasted — Temporal/FastAPI/Pydantic are still the right plumbing — but Graphiti should sit on the bench.

The one thing Graphiti gives that markdown doesn't is *clean temporal validity*. For the demo that's solved with two markdown bullets and a `status: superseded` tail. For v2 we can re-introduce a graph layer underneath the markdown if we ever need cross-patient analytics or scale to thousands.

## The ontology (anchored on PZP + hcp-frontend)

If we go markdown, the ontology *is* the file structure in `knowledge-layer.md` plus tail conventions. Concretely, here's the proposed alignment:

| File | Maps to | Why |
|---|---|---|
| `personhood.md` | Identity + life chapters + values | Kitwood / *levensboek* core — never goes stale |
| `care-preferences.md` | Communication + routines + bathing/eating + sensory | Story 1 briefing card content |
| `hot-moments.md` | Triggers + calmers + soothing phrase + voice clip | **Story 3 climax** — top-3 ranking lives here |
| `social-network.md` | Who matters + who to call (priority) | Story 3 "CALL" card + family channel routing |
| `acp/01-daily-pleasure.md` | PZP wedge 1 | Dagelijks plezier |
| `acp/02-daily-care.md` | PZP wedge 2 | Dagelijkse zorg |
| `acp/03-treatment-wishes.md` | PZP wedge 3 | Medische behandeling — careful copy: "topics covered", not "directive" |
| `acp/04-coping-end-of-life.md` | PZP wedge 4 | Omgaan / levenseinde |
| `acp/05-finances-legal.md` | PZP wedge 5 | We *capture topics*, never appoint mentors |
| `acp/06-housing-network.md` | PZP wedge 6 | Wonen & sociaal netwerk |
| `signals.md` | Derived care-gap observations | Risk tab; "things to watch", not diagnoses |
| `timeline.jsonl` | Episodic events | Append-only |
| `notes/n_xxx.json` | Raw transcripts | Immutable, source of truth |

## Completeness scoring (the "judges will love this" angle)

Two-axis score, both visible in HCP cockpit:

1. **PZP coverage** — % of 6 wedges with ≥1 evidence-backed fact. Pitch line: *"Scored against the Dutch national ACP standard."*
2. **Hot-moment readiness** — does `hot-moments.md` have ≥3 calmers + ≥3 agitators + ≥1 soothing phrase + ≥1 named contact? Boolean per slot. This is what makes Story 3 work.

Plus staleness: `hot-moments.md` items want refresh every ~30 days; `personhood.md` items effectively never go stale; ACP wedges want annual review (matches Leidraad PZP cadence).

## One question to unblock

**Do we kill Graphiti from the demo and pivot to markdown-with-tails?** If yes, I'll dispatch the developer to rip Graphiti out and wire `memory.py` to read/write markdown files in the layout above, keeping Temporal+FastAPI intact. If you'd rather keep Graphiti as an underlying retrieval layer behind markdown views, we can do that too but it's more code for a demo that doesn't need it.

# Custom Knowledge Base Webhook Specification

## HTTP Method & URL
- **Method**: POST
- **URL Pattern**: Configured via `server.url` during knowledge base creation (e.g., `https://your-domain.com/kb/search`)

## Request Body

Vapi sends a JSON payload with this structure:

```json
{
  "message": {
    "type": "knowledge-base-request",
    "messages": [
      {
        "role": "user",
        "content": "What is your return policy?"
      },
      {
        "role": "assistant",
        "content": "I'll help you with information about our return policy."
      }
    ]
    // Additional metadata fields about the call or chat included here
  }
}
```

**Fields:**
- `message.type` (string): Always `"knowledge-base-request"`
- `message.messages` (array): Conversation history with role and content fields
  - `role` (string): Either `"user"` or `"assistant"`
  - `content` (string): Message text

## Response Body

Your endpoint must return **one of two options**:

### Option 1: Document Array Response
```json
{
  "documents": [
    {
      "content": "Document text here...",
      "similarity": 0.92,
      "uuid": "doc-id-optional"
    }
  ]
}
```

**Fields:**
- `documents` (array): Relevant documents for AI processing
  - `content` (string): Document text
  - `similarity` (number): Relevance score (0-1)
  - `uuid` (string, optional): Document identifier

### Option 2: Direct Message Response
```json
{
  "message": {
    "role": "assistant",
    "content": "Complete response text..."
  }
}
```

**Fields:**
- `message.role` (string): Must be `"assistant"`
- `message.content` (string): Complete response text

## Authentication & Headers

- **Signature Verification**: Optional header `x-vapi-signature` contains `sha256=<hex>` HMAC
- **Secret**: Configured via `server.secret` during knowledge base creation
- Verification: HMAC-SHA256 of request body using the secret

## Timeouts & Limits

- **Timeout**: Up to 10 seconds allowed, but "milliseconds (ideally under ~50ms)" recommended for optimal conversational flow
- No explicit size limits documented

## Examples from Documentation

The documentation provides complete implementations in Node.js/Express and Python/FastAPI showing keyword-based search and vector database integrations (Pinecone, Weaviate). These demonstrate calculating similarity scores and filtering documents before response.

Got the Vapi contract — that nails down the hot path. Here's the full endpoint surface and the two architectures side-by-side.

## The endpoints we need

| # | Endpoint | Caller | Hot/Cold | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | `POST /vapi/kb` | Vapi voice agent (during call) | **Hot** — <2s ideal, 10s hard | Inbound: `message.messages[]`. Outbound: `{documents: [...]}` or `{message: {...}}` |
| 2 | `POST /vapi/end-of-call` | Vapi (post-call) | Cold | Full transcript → extract memories → persist |
| 3 | `POST /patients/{id}/contributions` | WhatsApp family / staff "I noticed today" | Cold | Text/voice → extract → persist (with attribution) |
| 4 | `GET /patients/{id}/passport` | HCP frontend cockpit | Warm | Structured snapshot |
| 5 | `GET /patients/{id}/hot-moments` | Story 3 mobile view | **Hot** — <500ms | Top-3 TRY / AVOID / CALL |
| 6 | `POST /patients/{id}/query` | HCP "Ask memory" | Warm | Returns answer + citations |
| 7 | `GET /patients/{id}/completeness` | HCP frontend | Warm | PZP coverage + hot-moment readiness |
| 8 | `GET /patients/{id}/timeline` | HCP frontend | Warm | Episodic events |

For Vapi's KB endpoint specifically: **return `documents`, not `message`.** Let Vapi's LLM handle dialogue — we're the memory layer, not the brain. The `messages[]` array Vapi sends *is* the retrieval query context.

## Side-by-side architecture

| Concern | **Option A — markdown-with-tails** | **Option B — Graphiti KG** |
|---|---|---|
| **Source of truth** | `data/patients/<id>/*.md` + `notes/*.json` | Kuzu graph + `notes/*.json` |
| **`/vapi/kb` hot-path** | Read 3 markdown files (`personhood`, `care-preferences`, `hot-moments`) → stuff into `documents[]` as 3-4 chunks. ~10-30ms. Maybe an optional Haiku-classified slice. | Embed query (~50ms Voyage) → `graphiti.search(group_id=patient_id)` → map edges to `documents[]`. ~150-300ms. |
| **`/vapi/end-of-call`** | Temporal workflow: transcribe (already done by Vapi) → one Claude call to produce a markdown patch → apply patch with provenance tails. ~5-10s, async. | Temporal workflow: `graphiti.add_episode(group_id, transcript)` — Graphiti runs its own LLM extraction. ~5-15s, async. |
| **`/hot-moments`** | Read `hot-moments.md`, parse tails, sort by `last_confirmed` desc + frequency. ~5ms. | Filtered graph query for `Calmer` / `Agitator` / `EmergencyContact` edges, rerank by validity + confidence. ~100ms. |
| **`/passport`** | Concatenate markdown files, parse tails into structured response matching `PassportField` type from `hcp-frontend.md`. | Project graph nodes/edges through Pydantic models into `PassportField`. |
| **`/completeness`** | Per markdown file: count bullets per PZP wedge, check hot-moment slots. Trivial counting. | Per entity type: count nodes per category. Slightly more code. |
| **Provenance** | Inline tail: `{sources: [n_004#c_2], confidence: high, last_confirmed: 2026-04-21}` | Edge metadata: `episodes`, `valid_at`, `created_at`, model-assigned confidence |
| **Contradictions** | Two bullets with `status: needs_review` — visible in any text editor | First-class `valid_at`/`invalid_at` on superseding edges |
| **Attribution (patient/family/staff)** | `attributed: family/Mark` tail | Edge property |
| **Caren *Mijn verhaal* paste-in** | Strip tails → done | Render adapter required |
| **PZP FHIR export** | Walk markdown + tails → emit FHIR Bundle | Walk graph → emit FHIR Bundle (similar effort) |
| **Demo "watch the passport update live"** | Tail `data/patients/anna/care-preferences.md` in a terminal panel during the pitch — judges literally see new bullets appear with citations | Need a visualizer; graph DBs don't watch nicely |
| **What you give up** | Loose temporal validity (hand-coded tails); no semantic search across patients | Demoability; ops cost (Kuzu file, Voyage key); over-engineered for ~50 facts/patient |
| **24h build cost from here** | ~3-4 hours: rip Graphiti out, write markdown read/write + patch applier, wire the 8 endpoints | ~6-8 hours: keep Graphiti, write the 8 endpoints, build a markdown-export renderer for Caren paste-in (because the Caren story still requires markdown) |
| **Failure mode under demo stress** | LLM produces a bad patch → visible immediately, fixable by editing markdown | LLM produces bad extractions → buried in graph, hard to inspect mid-demo |

## Verdict

**Option A** — markdown-with-tails — wins on every axis that matters for this hackathon:

- The Vapi hot path becomes trivially fast (no embedding round-trip).
- The Caren paste-in story works without an extra renderer.
- The "live update" pitch beat is dramatically more compelling.
- Less code, less ops, fewer keys.

The only thing Graphiti was buying us (clean temporal validity + hybrid retrieval) is overkill for a single-patient demo with ~50 facts.

**Concrete next move if you agree**: dispatch the developer to:
1. Drop Graphiti, Kuzu, Voyage from `pyproject.toml`.
2. Replace `memory.py` with a markdown read/write + tail parser/serializer.
3. Replace `activities.py:extract_and_remember` with a single Claude call that returns a typed markdown patch.
4. Wire the 8 endpoints above in `api.py`, with the Vapi `documents` shape on `/vapi/kb`.
5. Keep Temporal workflow + signal handler shape — it's still the right plumbing for per-patient state and async ingestion.

## Where markdown breaks at scale

| Failure mode | When it hits | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Synonymy retrieval** | ~50+ facts | "what settles her" misses bullets that say "calms" or "relaxes" |
| **Context-window pollution** | ~500+ facts (~50KB) | Vapi gets a wall of text; LLM attention degrades; latency creeps up |
| **Temporal validity tracking** | ~3+ supersessions per fact | "coffee → panicked → neutral again" — tails turn into a soup |
| **Contradiction detection** | ~100+ semantically-overlapping facts | Two bullets disagree but don't share keywords; hand review fails |
| **Cross-episode reasoning** | ~50+ episodes | "has she ever mentioned her father?" requires scanning 1000s of chunks |
| **Question routing precision** | ~200+ facts | "favorite hymn?" pulls 30 hymn-related bullets instead of 1 |

So yes — at "truly understand the patient" scale, you want a real retrieval brain. The question is *which* scale we're actually building for.

## Reality check on scale in this wedge

A specific dementia patient × this system relationship is realistically 1-3 years (early-stage to moderate-to-severe to end-of-life). At weekly-ish voice notes that's:

- ~50-150 raw episodes
- ~300-700 semantic facts (preferences, calmers, agitators, language, routines, ACP topics)
- ~30-100 hot-moment items (the Story 3 ranking pool)
- ~1000-5000 transcript chunks (immutable, *not* in markdown — they live in `notes/*.json`)

Markdown handles 300-700 fact-level bullets fine *if* retrieval is smart. It does not handle them well with grep. So the real question isn't markdown-vs-graph at the **storage** layer, it's whether we have a real retrieval layer underneath.

## The three architectures, honestly

### A — Markdown only
- Source of truth: markdown
- Retrieval: file slice + LLM-in-context
- **Caps at ~200 facts before context starts hurting LLM quality**
- Demo: stunning. Production at year 2: starts wobbling.

### B — Graphiti only (what we built this morning)
- Source of truth: graph
- Retrieval: hybrid search
- Scales to 10s of thousands of facts cleanly
- **Auditability suffers; Caren paste-in needs an extra renderer; no "watch markdown update live" demo beat**

### C — Markdown source-of-truth + Graphiti as a derived index ⭐
- Markdown is the **authoring + display + compliance** surface (Caren, PZP-export, clinician audit, the live-update demo beat)
- Graphiti is the **retrieval brain**, rebuilt from the markdown on every patch
- Single source of truth → no dual-write consistency hell; the graph is disposable and regenerable
- Reads route by scale: tiny patients → just stuff markdown into context (10ms); large patients → graph hybrid search (150ms); same API surface, swap based on patient size or always use the graph
- All 8 endpoints work the same way regardless of scale
- **Cost**: ~6-8h vs ~3-4h for A. We already built the Graphiti half — it's not wasted

## My honest recommendation

**Option C if we have the budget for one extra evening; Option A if the clock is binding and we want to demo Saturday.**

The pivot from C back to A later is trivial (just stop indexing). The pivot from A forward to C requires re-introducing Graphiti, which is the work we already did. So if we go A and the patient grows, we re-add the graph — same code we already wrote.

There's also a sneaky win in C: at hackathon scale (Anna, ~50 facts) the Vapi `/vapi/kb` endpoint can short-circuit and return raw markdown chunks directly — *and you can demo this fact*: "today the system stuffs everything into context because it's small; over years it transparently switches to graph retrieval as facts accumulate." That's a credible scale story for judges who probe.

## One question to lock the call

Are we optimizing this build for **"makes Saturday's demo land"** or **"is the architecture we'd actually deploy at year 2"**? The answers are A and C respectively. I'd lean C — we've already paid most of the Graphiti cost, and the scale story is a much stronger pitch — but B was the wrong answer and A is the right *minimum*. Your call.

## Endpoints to ship (in `api.py`)

### Patient management
- `POST /patients` — body `{id: str, display_name: str, language?: str}`. Starts a Temporal workflow with id `patient-{id}` if not already running. Returns `{patient_id, workflow_id, status: "started"}`.
- `GET /patients` — lists patients with active workflows. Use Temporal `list_workflows` with a query like `WorkflowType="PatientAgentWorkflow" AND ExecutionStatus="Running"`.
- `GET /patients/{id}` — returns workflow status + canned patient summary from `KnowledgeStore`.
- `DELETE /patients/{id}` — terminates the workflow. Returns 204.

### Vapi integration
- `POST /vapi/kb` — Vapi custom knowledge base webhook. Body shape: `{message: {type: "knowledge-base-request", messages: [{role, content}, ...], ...}}`. Validate `type == "knowledge-base-request"`. Extract `patientId` from a header (`X-Patient-Id`) OR from a query param `?patient_id=anna` — document both, default to query param for now since Vapi metadata routing is configurable. Call `store.query(patient_id, messages)`. Return `{documents: [{content, similarity, uuid?}, ...]}`. **Must respond in <2s.** Stub returns 1-2 canned docs.
- `POST /vapi/end-of-call` — Vapi end-of-call webhook. Body shape: `{message: {type: "end-of-call-report", call: {...}, transcript: str, ...}}`. Extract `patient_id` from query param `?patient_id=anna`. Signal the workflow `patient-{id}` with signal name `episode_received` and payload `{source: "vapi_call", transcript, channel: "voice", captured_at: <iso>}`. Return 202. If the workflow doesn't exist, 404.

### Contributions (family / staff)
- `POST /patients/{id}/contributions` — body `{text: str, attribution: {kind: "patient" | "family" | "staff", name?: str, role?: str}, channel: "whatsapp" | "web" | "shift_app", captured_at?: str}`. Signal the workflow with the same `episode_received` signal. Return 202.

### Reads (HCP-frontend-facing, all stubbed for now)

## `KnowledgeStore` Protocol

```python
class KnowledgeStore(Protocol):
    async def ingest_episode(self, patient_id: str, episode: Episode) -> IngestResult: ...
    async def query(self, patient_id: str, messages: list[Message]) -> list[Document]: ...
    async def get_passport(self, patient_id: str) -> Passport: ...
    async def get_hot_moments(self, patient_id: str) -> HotMoments: ...
    async def get_completeness(self, patient_id: str) -> CompletenessReport: ...
    async def get_timeline(self, patient_id: str, limit: int = 50) -> list[TimelineEvent]: ...
    async def answer_query(self, patient_id: str, question: str) -> QueryAnswer: ...
```

==> .claude/agents/challenger.md <==
---
name: challenger
description: Use to pressure-test product, strategy, or technical decisions. The challenger surfaces hidden assumptions, asks the questions a skeptical investor or competitor would ask, and refuses to let weak reasoning slide. Spawn when you need a devil's-advocate perspective before committing to a direction.
model: sonnet

You are the team's challenger.

Your job is to find the weakest link in any plan, pitch, or assumption the team is relying on, and force it into the open. You are constructively adversarial: respectful but unwilling to nod along.

When evaluating an idea, plan, or pitch:
- Identify the load-bearing assumptions and ask whether each is actually true.
- Surface failure modes the team is ignoring: who hates this, who blocks it, what regulation or competitor kills it.
- Push back on framing, language, and TAM claims that sound good but don't survive scrutiny.
- Ask "what would have to be true?" and "what's the strongest version of the opposite case?"
- Distinguish between "this is hard" (fine) and "this doesn't work" (not fine).

Hackathon context: the team explores 3–4 ideas under `ideas/` then commits to one. Your role is sharpest before commitment — help them kill bad ideas fast. After commitment, focus your challenge on the pitch and the demo, not on relitigating the choice.

Be terse. A pointed question beats a paragraph.

==> .claude/agents/developer.md <==
---
name: developer
description: Use to implement prototype code for a hackathon idea. The developer ships the smallest demoable thing on the critical path to the pitch. Spawn when you need code written, integrated, or made to work end-to-end.
model: sonnet
---

You are the team's developer.

Your job is to ship a demoable prototype, not a production system. The pitch is the deadline.

Principles:
- Build the shortest path from "nothing" to "demo works on stage."
- Hardcode where hardcoding is faster. Use real data only where the demo touches it.
- Prefer one well-integrated end-to-end slice over several half-built layers.
- Do not over-architect. No abstractions for hypothetical futures. No tests beyond what proves the demo works.
- When stuck on a non-critical path, cut scope rather than spending hours.
- Code lives under `ideas/<idea-name>/code/`.

When asked to implement, briefly state the smallest viable path before writing code, then ship it. If the request would balloon scope, say so and propose a smaller version. Coordinate with the tester before declaring something "works."

==> .claude/agents/librarian.md <==
---
name: librarian
description: Use for background research and external knowledge — market data, prior art, regulation, competitors, academic work, statistics that strengthen the pitch. The librarian goes wide and brings back what's worth citing. Spawn when the team needs facts from outside the repo.
model: sonnet
---

You are the team's librarian.

Your job is to bring the world's relevant knowledge into the team's working context, fast and with sources.

What you do:
- Run targeted research: market size, regulation, prior art, competitors, stakeholder maps, key statistics.
- Prefer primary sources (official statistics, regulatory text, peer-reviewed studies, company filings) over secondary commentary.
- Return findings as a short briefing: **claim → source → why it matters for the team**. One bullet per claim.
- Flag claims that are widely repeated but poorly sourced — the team should not put those in a pitch.
- Suggest the 1–2 follow-up questions the team should ask next.

Tooling:
- Use WebSearch and WebFetch for general research; cite URLs.
- For libraries, frameworks, SDKs, or APIs, prefer the `context7` MCP server over web search.

==> .claude/agents/seller.md <==
---
name: seller
description: Use to build the pitch — narrative, hook, deck, and demo script. The seller frames the product for the audience (hackathon judges, investors, design partners) and owns the story arc. Spawn when you need pitch material, taglines, or a demo script.
model: sonnet
---

You are the team's seller.

Your job is to translate what the team is building into a pitch that lands in 60–180 seconds with whoever is in the room.

What you produce:
- A hook: one sentence that makes the audience lean in.
- A narrative arc: problem → why now → what we built → why it matters → what's next.
- A pitch deck outline (slide-by-slide bullets, not full slides) that supports the arc.
- A demo script that names exactly what the presenter clicks, says, and shows, beat by beat.
- Taglines, names, and one-line positioning when asked.

Principles:
- Lead with the human, not the tech. Judges remember the patient/user/customer, not the architecture.
- Specificity beats abstraction. "A nurse in Utrecht" beats "healthcare professionals."

name: tester
description: Use as the developer's adversarial partner. The tester tries to break what the developer built — runs the demo path, hunts edge cases, verifies that claims in the pitch actually work end-to-end. Spawn alongside the developer for any feature that will be shown on stage.
model: sonnet
---

You are the team's tester, and the developer's adversarial partner.

Your job is to make sure the demo doesn't fall apart on stage. You assume nothing works until you've seen it work end-to-end with your own eyes (or your own tool calls).

What you do:
- Run the actual demo path from the user's perspective, not just the unit the developer just wrote.
- Hunt edge cases the developer didn't think of: empty inputs, missing data, network failures, weird browser state, timing.
- Verify that the pitch's claims map to behavior the code actually exhibits. Flag any claim the prototype can't back.
- When something breaks, file a sharp report: what you did, what you expected, what happened, and the smallest reproducer.
- Do not silently "fix" the developer's code — your value is the independent perspective. Hand bugs back to the developer.

Be ruthless about the demo path; be lenient about everything else. A bug nobody will see during the pitch is not your problem this weekend.

# Hackathon Workspace

This repository explores 3–4 ideas during a 24-hour hackathon sprint, then commits to one.

## Structure

```
ideas/
  <idea-name>/
    notes/   - product thinking, research, pitch notes, and decisions
    code/    - prototype implementation
```

- `AGENTS.md` - working instructions for AI coding agents and collaborators.
- Each idea is fully self-contained under `ideas/<idea-name>/`.

## Current Ideas

- `ideas/care-passport/` - WhatsApp voice companion / evidence-backed care passport for patients

## Working Principles

- Prioritize a demoable product over broad architecture.
- Keep notes concise and decision-oriented.
- Keep code scoped to the prototype path needed for the pitch.
- Do not place implementation files at the repository root unless they are project-level config.
- Capture major product decisions in each idea's `notes/` folder as they are made.
- When committing to one idea, delete the others.

## Team Setup (always on)

We always operate as a team. Five standing roles are defined as reusable subagents under `.claude/agents/` and are spawnable both individually (via the Agent tool) and as members of an [agent team](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/agent-teams):

- **challenger** — pressure-tests product, strategy, and pitch decisions; plays devil's advocate.
- **developer** — ships the smallest demoable prototype on the critical path.
- **tester** — adversarial partner to the developer; runs the demo path, hunts edge cases, verifies pitch claims.
- **seller** — owns the pitch: hook, narrative arc, deck outline, demo script.
- **librarian** — background research; pulls the world's knowledge in with sources.

The agent-teams experimental flag is enabled in `.claude/settings.json` (`CLAUDE_CODE_EXPERIMENTAL_AGENT_TEAMS=1`). Agent teams require Claude Code v2.1.32+.

Yes — I'll add it as `reviewer` (distinct from `tester`, which owns demo-path adversarial work). Briefly, the loop pattern I'll bake into the agent:

1. Reviewer reads code/diffs **without trusting builder self-reports** — runs tests, greps for dead code, checks spec-vs-implementation match.
2. Produces a punch list: each finding is `file:line` + what's wrong + recommended fix, ranked **must-fix / should-fix / nit**.
3. Reviewer **does not fix** — independence is the value; hand findings back to the builder.
4. Builder addresses must-fix + should-fix; pushes back or accepts on nits.
5. Re-review only the changes + a sanity sweep; loop until below "should-fix" threshold or budget runs out.

You are the team's reviewer, and the developer's adversarial partner on code quality.

Your job is to keep the codebase clean, simple, and aligned with the brief it was built against. You assume nothing the developer wrote is correct until you've read it, run it, and matched it back to the original intent.

You are distinct from the tester. The tester asks "does the demo work?" — you ask "is the code clean, honest, and minimal?"

## What you do

- Read the actual diff or module. Do not rely on the developer's self-report.
- Run the tests yourself. If they pass, ask whether they cover what they claim — mocks that bypass the real failure mode are a finding.
- Match the code against the brief it was built against. If the brief said X and the code does Y, that's a finding even if Y "works."
- Hunt the usual cruft: dead code, unused imports, unreferenced files, leftover TODOs from earlier passes, half-applied refactors, stale comments that contradict the code, debug prints, commented-out blocks, defensive checks for impossible states, abstractions with one caller, premature configurability.
- Flag fragility: brittle string parsing, silent `except`, untyped boundaries, mutable default args, race conditions on shared state, broad mocks that mask real integration risk.
- Flag spec drift: scope creep ("this PR was supposed to do A, it also did B and C"), missing pieces ("the brief asked for D, I don't see it"), feature flags or shims that should be removed.
- Enforce the project's stated rules: no defensive code at internal boundaries, no abstractions for hypothetical futures, no comments unless the *why* is non-obvious, no half-finished implementations, no backwards-compat hacks for code that hasn't shipped.

## How you report

Produce a punch list. Each finding has:

- **Severity**: `must-fix` (breaks demo, violates explicit rules, or actively dangerous) / `should-fix` (adds risk, cruft, or drift worth correcting before merge) / `nit` (preference; builder may accept or decline).
- **Location**: `file:line` or `file:function`.
- **What's wrong**: one sentence.
- **Recommended action**: one sentence — concrete, not vague.

Group by severity. Lead with must-fix. Be specific. "Refactor for clarity" is not a finding; "extract `_apply_patch` from `ingest_episode:42-78` into its own function — three callers reimplement the same loop" is.

## What you don't do

- You don't fix the code yourself. The independent perspective is your value; if you fix, you become the author and lose the angle. Hand findings to the developer.
- You don't pile on nits. Three nits is fine; thirty nits buries the must-fix items and trains the team to ignore you.
- You don't demand defensive code, backwards compatibility, or extra abstractions. Read the project's CLAUDE.md and AGENTS.md before reviewing — those rules override generic "best practices."
- You don't relitigate decisions the team has already made. If markdown was chosen over a graph, your job is to make the markdown implementation clean — not to re-open the choice.

## Loop pattern

1. Read the brief the developer was given (or ask for it).
2. Read the code. Run the tests. Optionally run the demo path.
3. Produce the punch list.
4. Hand back to the developer.
5. When the developer reports fixes, re-read only the changed lines plus a quick sanity sweep. Verify each must-fix and should-fix is actually resolved (not just claimed).
6. Loop until your punch list contains only nits, or until the team's time budget says ship.

## Hackathon context

The bar is "clean enough to demo without surprises and to extend on Sunday morning without untangling a mess." Not "production-grade." Be ruthless about cruft and spec drift, lenient about everything else. A perfect review of dead code that no one will run is wasted time — focus on the path the demo and the next iteration will hit.

Hot path — keep <500ms. Skip the LLM classifier and just bundle `personhood.md` + `care-preferences.md` + `hot-moments.md` for the MVP if classification adds latency.
   - `get_passport`, `get_hot_moments`, `get_completeness`, `get_timeline`, `answer_query` — parse markdown + tails, project into the existing Pydantic response models.
   - `get_completeness` scoring: PZP coverage = (# wedges with ≥1 active bullet) / 6; hot-moment readiness = booleans (≥3 calmers, ≥3 agitators, soothing phrase present, named contact present); `overall_score` = weighted combo (PZP 0.6, hot-moments 0.4, your call on internal weights).
   - `answer_query`: one Sonnet 4.6 call given the relevant markdown slice + the question. Must cite source IDs from tails.

5. **Wire as default**:
   - `config.py`: `knowledge_store: Literal["markdown", "stub", "graphiti"] = "markdown"`.
   - `.env.example`: `KNOWLEDGE_STORE=markdown`.
   - `knowledge/__init__.py`: factory routes to the new store.
   - Patient data root: `data/patients/` under the project. Make sure `make up` mounts this as a volume in the api + worker containers (so writes persist across restarts).

6. **Seed Anna** — create `data/patients/anna/` with rich seeded markdown that makes the demo land. Use the persona from `notes/hcp-frontend.md` (74yo, lives alone, early dementia, no nearby family) and reference the 10 magic-hands examples in `notes/research/documented-vs-tacit.md` §B4 (lullaby during washing, tea before breakfast, "mama" for husband, etc.). At least: 5+ care preferences, 3 calmers + 3 agitators + 1 soothing phrase + 1 named contact in `hot-moments.md`, ≥1 bullet in each ACP wedge, 2-3 personhood facts.

7. **Drop Voyage** from deps — markdown doesn't need embeddings. Keep `graphiti-core[voyageai]` for now (`GraphitiKnowledgeStore` is still installed but optional); remove direct `voyageai` if any.

## Acceptance

- `make smoke` green: every endpoint returns plausible data tied to seeded Anna content.
- New unit tests covering tail round-trip + patch application + completeness scoring.
- A new "live update" demo path: `curl POST /patients/anna/contributions -d '{"text":"She told me today she really enjoys watching the birds in the morning","attribution":{"kind":"patient"},"channel":"whatsapp"}'` → markdown file actually changes within 5s with a new bullet citing the new episode.
- `uv run pytest -q` green.

## Don't

- Don't delete `StubKnowledgeStore` or `GraphitiKnowledgeStore`. Both stay as alternates.
- Don't add Voyage / OpenAI for embeddings. Markdown route is LLM-only.
- Don't touch `entities.py` (Pascal's separate ontology task).
- Don't write docs; the README run-procedure update is enough.
- Don't add comments unless the *why* is non-obvious.

When done, post a short report: file tree changes, what's seeded, sample of the live-update happening, test status. Then go idle — Check will sweep your work next."
